chunks.summary: ad-saturated world

beats.narration: Ads are everywhere now.
Which means everybody is trying to make theirs work better.

chunks.summary: analyst looks at normal ad metrics

beats.narration: And most ad-analysis tools do something very reasonable.

camera.reason: establish the analyst at their desk

beats.narration: They look at click-through rate, watch time, conversion rate, and return on ad spend.

camera.reason: narrator lists metrics shown on the laptop screen — zoom to the chart

beats.narration: That is the normal way to judge whether an ad is doing its job.

camera.reason: pull back for the observational deadpan line

chunks.summary: analyst pivots — stranger question

beats.narration: Then we wanted to ask a much stranger question.
What if a model looked at a video
and tried to predict brain-response patterns from it?

chunks.summary: brain patterns, deadpan

beats.narration: Because apparently just watching the ad was no longer enough.
Brain-response patterns. You know. Casually.

chunks.summary: TRIBE is not a chatbot

beats.narration: That is what TRIBE is about.
It is not a chatbot.
It is not "ask the model a question and get a neat paragraph back."

chunks.summary: video/audio/text → brain activity flow

beats.narration: TRIBE takes video, audio, and text,
and predicts patterns of brain activity the content might be associated with.
Which is a sentence that sounds fake right up until you realize Meta actually built it.

chunks.summary: so we tested it

beats.narration: So we wanted to test it properly.
Could TRIBE tell us anything useful about ad creative in a real workflow?

chunks.summary: Meta quiet in a noisy AI landscape

beats.narration: And part of what made it worth looking at was the timing.
At that point Meta did not exactly feel like the company setting the tone in the public AI conversation.
A lot of the energy was elsewhere.
A lot of the attention was going to more familiar product shapes.

chunks.summary: Meta shows up with something different

beats.narration: So seeing Meta show up with something this different felt notable.

chunks.summary: back to the practical question

beats.narration: But the real question was still practical: does any of this belong in a workflow?

chunks.summary: research vs workflow — two different standards

beats.narration: Because interesting research and useful workflow are two different standards.
Is this a weird and ambitious piece of research?
Obviously.

chunks.summary: the harder question — survives real use

beats.narration: The harder question is whether it stays reasonable once it meets an actual machine, an actual file, and an actual wait time.

chunks.summary: the plan — a simple A vs B experiment

beats.narration: So the plan was simple.
Run a real A-versus-B experiment and see what survived contact with reality.

chunks.summary: setup required before any result

beats.narration: And before there was any result, there was setup.

chunks.summary: gated access, auth issues, tribev2 install

beats.narration: There was gated model access.
There were Hugging Face authentication issues.
There was the discovery that tribev2 was not a normal package you could just install and move on with your life.

chunks.summary: environment mess, computer not persuaded

beats.narration: There were environment mismatches and dependency problems.
There was the broader experience of taking a research stack and politely asking it to behave like a product.
It was not immediately persuaded.

chunks.summary: moved to Colab, still slow

beats.narration: So the workflow moved into Google Colab on a Tesla T4.
Which sounds encouraging until you remember that it runs and this is practical are not the same sentence.

chunks.summary: setup over, experiment begins

beats.narration: Eventually the setup friction gave way to the actual experiment.
Two videos went through the TRIBE pipeline in a blind A-versus-B comparison.

chunks.summary: measurements: hook strength, peak timing, cortical regions

beats.narration: The output was reduced into measurements that were easier to reason about.
Hook strength.
Overall average response and peak ROI response.
Peak timing.
And a set of cortical regions used as rough proxies for attention, salience, control, evaluation, and higher-level meaning.

chunks.summary: plain english — which video gets stronger attention?

beats.narration: In plain English, the question was simple: which video seems more likely to trigger a stronger pay-attention-to-this response?

chunks.summary: not mind-reading — not reading real brains

beats.narration: Not in a mystical way.
Not in a literal mind-reading way.
TRIBE is not reading a real person's brain.

chunks.summary: predicting patterns from video content

beats.narration: It is predicting response patterns from the content of the video.
That distinction matters, because otherwise this turns into fake sci-fi very quickly.

chunks.summary: pipeline produced a real, non-random answer

beats.narration: And once the pipeline finally got through the setup and the waiting, it did actually produce a real answer.
The output was not random noise.

chunks.summary: TRIBE favored Video A across hook, peak, regions

beats.narration: TRIBE-style signals favored Video A.
Video A showed the stronger early hook.
It showed stronger peak responses too.
Several target regions also leaned higher for A.

chunks.summary: if allowed to vote, TRIBE had opinions

beats.narration: If the model had been allowed to cast a confident vote, it would have had opinions.

chunks.summary: experiment gets useful — business result goes the other way

beats.narration: And this is where the experiment became actually useful.
Because the business result went the other way.

chunks.summary: THE REVEAL — ROAS mismatch (Video B won in market)

beats.narration: Video B had ROAS above three, while Video A was closer to two and a half.
So the model favorite and the market winner were not the same thing.
The model-favored video lost in the market.

chunks.summary: the mismatch is the real story

beats.narration: That does not mean the model output was nonsense.
It means the mismatch is the real story.

chunks.summary: not 'can it produce' — 'what kind of output'

beats.narration: Because now the question is not can this model produce output.
Clearly it can.
The more important question is what kind of output this actually is.

chunks.summary: useful as research/interpretation layer, not winner picker

beats.narration: TRIBE looks more useful as a research and interpretation layer,
something that surfaces structured differences between creatives,
and helps produce better hypotheses.

chunks.summary: not a standalone winner picker

beats.narration: That is not the same thing as a standalone winner picker.

chunks.summary: local fragile, Colab limited

beats.narration: And the runtime had opinions too.
Running the stack locally on a sixteen-gigabyte MacBook was slow and fragile.
Running it in Colab on a Tesla T4 worked, but only in a limited sense.

chunks.summary: 3.5 hours per short clip

beats.narration: Each short clip still took about three and a half hours to encode.

chunks.summary: not a quick scoring pass — 'rest of day has plans'

beats.narration: So this was less quick scoring pass,
and more excellent, the rest of the day has made other plans.

chunks.summary: practical tools need to be usable — this isn't

beats.narration: Practical tools do not only need to be smart. They need to be usable.
And this workflow is not ready to behave like a casual product feature.

chunks.summary: not laptop, not low-cost VPS — needs dedicated GPU

beats.narration: So if something like this ever becomes a real tool,
it is not running on a laptop.
it is not living on a low-cost VPS.
It needs a dedicated GPU-backed path.

chunks.summary: operational takeaway as important as the model takeaway

beats.narration: And definitely not a workflow pretending this is some light little background check you run before lunch.
That makes the operational takeaway almost as important as the model takeaway.

chunks.summary: TRIBE v2 is non-commercial license

beats.narration: There is one more constraint sitting quietly on top of all of that.
TRIBE v2 is under a non-commercial license.
So even if the workflow were faster, this exact model still is not something you casually drop into a product and call finished.

chunks.summary: back to the original question

beats.narration: Which brings us back to the original question.
Can this help judge whether a video ad is good or bad?

chunks.summary: structured signals — worth studying, not trusting alone

beats.narration: It can produce structured signals that are interesting enough to study,
but not reliable enough to trust on their own, and not cheap enough to treat like a normal scoring layer.

chunks.summary: not a clean cinematic success — but a real answer

beats.narration: That is not the clean cinematic success story people usually want from this kind of experiment.
It is, however, a real answer.

chunks.summary: the gap between model and market — the real lesson

beats.narration: And in some ways it is the better answer, because the gap between model reward and market reward is the part worth taking seriously.

chunks.summary: the gap is where useful questions begin

beats.narration: That gap is where the useful questions begin.
What exactly is it seeing?
What is it missing, and when does that difference matter?

chunks.summary: better place to end than a fake victory lap

beats.narration: That is a much better place for an experiment to end than a fake victory lap.
